title: 第 35 節
en: It is much the same in mythology. If we are told, for instance, as Kuhn has pointed out, that Rudra's hair is represented as braided and tied up in a knot (kapardin), and that Apollon's hair was represented as arranged in the same manner (a/ce/xxeKo/x^s), this is, first of all, not quite correct, and secondly, it would prove too much \ All that we are justified and all, I believe, that Kuhn really meant was that a god represented as moving rapidly through the air, like Apollon, would naturally be conceived as having his hair flowing freely about his head, and this is how he is represented in the oldest statues, while in the case of 1 K. Z., iii, 335 ; and von Schroeder, K. Z., xxix, 227.

Rudra his kaparda or top-knot, formed like a shell, may be no more than a repetition of the hair-knot affected by his worshippers as, later on, by the worshippers of $iva \ If Kuhn compared the epithet of Apollon, Loxias, oblique, with the epithet of Rudra, vanku, athwart, he could hardly have taken Loxias in the sense of \o£6<;, crooked, as alluding to the crooked or deceitful meaning of Apollon's oracles. This interpretation of Loxias had been rejected long ago by Otfried Miiller, who showed that Apollon's sister, Artemis, was likewise called Loxo, though she never was accused of having uttered oracles, whether straight or crooked. What Kuhn really meant was that vaiiku, as applied to Rudra, was probably intended for rushing violently athwart the air, and that Loxias had the same physical meaning when originally applied to Apollon. But many gods are represented as moving or rushing, without being therefore identical, nor is vaiiku an epithet exclusively belonging to Rudra.

The same may be said about Kuhn's remark that Apollon played the lyre and that there was a musical instrument called rudri, possibly the same word as Xvpa. Rudri is a very late word and little authenticated in Sanskrit literature. The musical character of Apollon as leader of the Muses is far too peculiarly Greek to allow of comparison with Rudra, whose music, if any, was more like the whistling and howling of the storm than the strains of the lyre. Still, if Kuhn wanted only to refer to the physical character of Rudra, he might have 1 M. M., S. B. E., vol. xxxii, p. 424.

Vi] APOLLON. 705 strengthened his case by a reference to the Rudras, plur., that is the Maruts, storm-winds, who in the Veda are constantly represented as singers (arkin) '. It is true that both Apollon and Rudra have bow and arrows, but so have other gods both in Vedic and Greek mythology. Agni constantly shoots his arrows, Artemis, Herakles, and Odysseus were famous as archers, though with the ancient Greeks the use of the bow was not considered so honourable as sword or lance.

That both Apollon and 'Rudra are represented as the best of physicians is certainly startling, and shows that, as we shall see, there was some truth in Kuhn's theory that Rudra and Apollon were cognate gods. What Kuhn omitted to consider were the many points of difference between Apollon and Rudra, which are equally important for our purpose.

We now turn to examine von Schroeder's view that there was a deity, Apohon-Agni, that is to say, that what the Vedic poets called Agni the Greeks called Apollon. This view contains undoubtedly a certain amount of truth, if only Agni is taken, as he so often appears in the Veda, not simply as the fire, the ordinary ignis, but as the heavenly fire or the heavenly light. How close the connection between these two manifestations of light on the hearth and in the East is, has been shown again and again. If therefore v. Schroeder would take Agni not in the ordinary sense of fire, but as the fiery sun, more particularly the rising sun, I should feel in full agreement with him. Both Rudra and Agni, as presented to us in the Veda, are too far developed to 1 M. M., Vedic Hymns, S. B. E., vol. xxxii, p. 95.

lend themselves to a comparison with so thoroughly Greek a deity as Phoibos Apollon. We must go behind the Vedic gods before we can hope to find the roots of the ideas which on Greek soil produced Apollon, on Indian soil Agni. The constant use of apa-var with reference to the daily revelation wrought by the rising sun, shows that behind Apollon and behind the various personalities of the rising sun, there lay the common idea of a revealing or morning deity, the germ also of the Devir Dvara/i, or the Everlasting Gates of the psalmist. If from this central idea there sprang secondary thoughts connected with the name of Apollon in Greece, or with the name of Agni, Surya, or Rudra in Sanskrit, all coincidences, even the most minute and apparently unmeaning, will be welcome as showing that the mythological growth which began in Pre -Vedic times followed the same natural course in India and in Greece, presenting many similarities, but not preventing dissimilarities also between the gods of Greece and the gods of India. Apollon cannot be Pudra, nor Agni, as little as the Greek Archon is the Buddhist Arhan, or even Latin rex the Sk. rkg.

Pudra was originally a storm-god, whatever may be said to the contrary. If the Maruts were stormgods, and even in modern Sanskrit marut means wind, we must not forget that the Maruts are called not only the sons of Pudra, but Pudras themselves.

When we read that Pudra is the most beautiful among the gods, that he holds bow and arrow, that he is the lord of songs (gatha-pati), and that he is the best of physicians and able also to remove the evil that man has committed (VI, 74, 3), we seem to have a god before us who certainly reminds us of Vl] APOLLON. 707 Apollon, but when Rudra is called the fierce god with strong limbs, who attacks like a wild beast, when he is called the red boar, when in the Atharvaveda his belly is said to be blue, and his back red, Ave can no longer recognise in hirn the features of Apollon even in his most terrible moods. Still less would it be possible to identify Rudra and the Rudras with mere Fauns and Satyrs, with spirits of the forest and the hills l, for Rudra is a majestic god, he shines like the bright sun (I, 43, 5), he is the lord of this wide world, and divine power will never depart from him (II, 33, 9).
Greek and Italian Gods.

If it is impossible, nay irrational, to attempt to identify Vedic and Greek gods after they have assumed a definite individuality in India or Greece, it is equally so with gods that have assumed a definite character in Greece and in Italy. What Comparative Mythology can do is to try to go behind such gods, and to discover, if possible, their common background far away from Italy and Greece ; but we cannot, and we should not attempt to trace what is specifically Greek in Italian mythology.

I have always admired Roscher's essay on Apollon and Mars, published many years ago in 1873. It is full of profound learning and clear discernment, and has been followed up by other works worthy of that brilliant beginning. Nor do I deny even now that there are many things which these two gods share in common, but I cannot believe that they started from one and the same germ. Apollon was certainly, as Roscher has fully shown, a god of light, a <E>ch/3o9, 1 Olden berg, Rel. des Veda, p. 223.
708 GREEK AND ITALIAN GODS. [chap.

a Au/ceios, AvKrjyevrjs, AlyXrfTrjs, 'Ewt'o?, and "Evavpos, he was likewise a god of the year, that is, a solar god, he was more particularly a god of spring and summer, he was a fighting-god, and, like most gods, a reputed ancestor of families, of clans, and of whole tribes, a patron of cities, a leader of colonies. All this springs naturally from his solar character. He was an apa-varyan, but he had not become individualised in any of the individual deities of the Veda, as we know it. If, therefore, we supposed with Poscher that for a certain time this as yet undefined Apavaryan was carried on in the same bed of mythological thought as an Italian god, who came to be called, or actually was called Mars, we should have to grant a long-continued unseparated existence of what is called, and, I believe, wrongly called, the Graeco-Italic branch of the Aryan family, and a development of a Graeco-Italic mythology different in its peculiar character from all other branches of Aryan mythology. And while this was going on, we should be called upon to believe that the name of one of these cognate Graeco-Italic deities became Apollon in Greece, but Mars in Italy, that the name changed, but that the substance remained the same. I do not deny that gods may be the same in substance, though different in name, but I doubt the possibility of the existence of any Aryan deities without a name. We saw a clear instance in the case of Varuna and Ahura Mazda, and Professor Roscher has mentioned several similar cases, such as the Erinyes, the Eumenides, Arae and Semnae ; Plouton, Hades, Klymenos ; Hebe and Ganymedes ; Aphrodite, Kypris, and Kythereia ; Persephone, Kore, and Phersephatta. But these cases are not Vi] GREEK AND ITALIAN GODS. 709 all to the point. We must distinguish between cases where a divine individuality receives a number of names, and where one or the other of them becomes in time an independent name. If Aphrodite or Aphrogeneia was called Kythereia and Kypris, because she received special worship in these islands, and if afterwards she seemed under these names, whether they had been mere epithets or independent names, to become a new deity, we can clearly see that what may be called her substance remained the same, though her name was changed. But this is not what Prof. Roscher supposes to have been the case with Apollon and Mars. He does not hold, if I understand him right, that Apollon ever received the epithet of Mars, or Mars that of Apollon, but rather that the same as yet anonymous deity received the name of Apollon in Greece, and of Mars in Italy, that Mars and Apollon were in fact dialectic synonyms. Here we must before all things try to think the matter out clearly. We saw that every deity begins its existence with its name. I hold that even the sun and moon did not exist — for men as thinking beings — till they had been named ; but at all events Helios and Selene could have had no existence till they had been created by the 6vo/jiaTo0eT7]<;.

There never was a Helios or an Apollon except in the mind of him who gathered up certain sensuous impressions, comprehended or conceived them and named them from one of their prominent characteristics, and thus called a revealing god, an Apollon, into existence. After that, two things may happen. Apollon may receive a number of epithets, and some of them, such as Hekatos, far-reaching, or yiO GREEK AND ITALIAN GODS. [chap.

Hekatebolos, the Far-darter, or Delios, bright or born in Delos, may assume a kind of mythological independence ; or secondly the same impressions which called forth the name of Apollon may, without any reference to that name, call forth another name and another god so near to Apollon that the two could not be kept apart, and after a time became one. In this way Phoibos, the brilliant, was so clearly meant for the same concept as Apollon, that the two were joined as Phoibos Apollon, and Phoibos ceased to be a separate deity and was afterwards accepted as a mere epithet of Apollon, like Hekatos, though really produced by an independent process.

If then we ask what was the relation of the Graeco-Italic Apollon to the Graeco-Italic Mars, we cannot possibly accept one as the epithet of the other, but we can only hold that the same concept which received the name of Apollon received likewise the name of Mars. Can that be proved ?
Apollon and Mars.

Professor Boscher seems not to have been aware that the identity of Apollon and Mars has been maintained by the Greeks themselves. Plutarch (Fragm. p. 15) argues that if two goddesses are represented as mothers of the same child there must have been a common element in both. Leto, therefore, he thinks, cannot be different from Here, because Leto is the mother of Artemis, and Artemis, when called Eileithyia, is the daughter of Here. He then goes on to argue that as Ares and Apollon have the same character (Swa/xis), the one being the son of Here, the other of Leto, it follows once more vi] APOLLON AND MARS. 7II that Leto and Here must be taken as the same goddess.

But this would hardly satisfy Professor Roscher, for although the Romans were led to identify their Mars with the Greek Ares, he has rightly pointed out (p. 14) that this may have been done at a later time and under a mistaken view, and that these two gods had become different in character long before they met again at Rome. The question therefore is, whether the real Roman Mars shows evidence of a solar origin and of a former identity with Apollon, so that we should have to take the Greek Apollon and the Latin Mars as mere synonyms, meant from the beginning for the same object.
That Mars was one of the bright, light-bringing gods is shown by his having been worshipped as Leuc-etius, or Louc-etius, while Apollon's epithets were Av/ccuo?, Av/cei09, AvK-qyevrj^, &c.

But though this epithet would certainly prove that Mars belonged to the bright gods, the devas, it would only prove that he was one of the devas, a bright being, not that he was a solar god or identical with Apollon. Lucetius, as an epithet, belongs as much to Jupiter as to Mars, if not more. Festus said, ' Lucetium Jovem appellabant quod eum lucis esse causam credebant,' and according to Macrobius it was this very Jupiter Lucetius whom the Salii celebrated in their song, and not Mars l. The wife of this Jupiter Lucetius was called Lucetia and Lucina, while the wife of Mars was Neria.
But though we have to surrender the strongest argument for the identity of Mars and Apollon, 1 Hartung, Die Religion der Eomer, ii, 9.

namely his name of Lucetius, we may retain nevertheless the fact that Mars belonged to the Devas, and that he was supposed to bring light and, as we shall see, the warmth of spring also. His was therefore the month Martius, or March, the beginning of spring in Italy. He was in fact a thoroughly Italian deity, and in all his features a god of Italian peasants. To begin with his name, I still hold, for reasons explained in my Introduction to the Vedic Hymns, vol. xxxii, p. xxiv, that Mars, Martis, corresponds to Marut, the storm-wind, and meant originally the smasher or pounder, like Pilumnus with his pilum, and Picumnus, connected with the Picus Martius (the woodpecker). The old derivation of Marut from mar, to be brilliant, labours under great disadvantages. If such a root is Aryan at all, it certainly has left no offspring in Sanskrit, not even mariAi. The derivation from mar also in the sense of dying is untenable in Sanskrit, because there is no allusion in the hymns addressed to the Maruts to their representing the spirits of the departed, and hardly any as to their conducting these spirits to their last resting-place. We seldom find so striking a coincidence as between the Vedic sardha maruta, the troop of the Maruts, and the Umbrian cerfo Martio, which really ought to have settled the question of the connection between Mars and Marut. That the wind or the hurricane should be raised to the rank of a supreme god is very natural among peasants and shepherds, and the same process can be watched not only in the case of Hurakan1, but likewise in that of Wuotan 2. That the winds possess all the 1 M. M., Natural Keligion, p. 453.
2 L. c. p. 324. Physical Keligion, pp. 314, 324.

Vl] APOLLON AND MAES. 713 qualities which appear in the warlike character of Mars, that the Maruts are in fact represented in the Veda as fighting-men in full armour, can easily be seen in the volume l which gives a translation of all the hymns addressed to the Maruts which we possess. But that Mars as the representative of the winds should have been regarded as beneficent to fields and meadows and forests is more difficult to explain. Our idea of March winds is taken only from what is painful in them. But in southern climates the March winds mark the return of the sun and of sunny weather. The winds themselves are felt to be necessary for sweeping the fields, for driving away whatever remains of fallen leaves or snow or dust, for drying the damp soil and purifying the air, for cleaning in fact the stable of Augeias. Hence Deverra, the sweeper, was a fit companion of Pilumnus, while Picus or Picumnus was credited with the introduction of manuring, both being friends and companions, sometimes the very deputies of Mars.

The son of Picus and father of Latinus and the tutelary deity of agriculture was Faunus. Faunus and the Fauni were afterwards identified with the Greek Pan and Panes. Their name is generally derived from favere, to protect, but as there is a class of beings called Dhuni in the Veda, which means shakers or shouters, Fau-nus is more likely a derivative of dhu or dhev, from which OveXXa as well as fumus, than of the unmeaning favere, to be propitious. The form Mavors by the side of Mars is difficult to explain. Maurs occurs in inscriptions, and is taken by Mommsen as representing Mars, the 1 S. B. E., vol. xxxii.
-714 APOLLON AND MARS. [chap.

murderer. Ritschl takes it as representing Mavors ; but what is Mavors ? Unless it could be taken as a mere elongation of Maurs (like Favonius of Faunus), I see no possibility of explaining it. Corssen explains it as a contraction of mag-vors, and takes mag as a possible Latin form of fia-x7)- This would give the meaning of battle-turner, like rpoTralos in Greek. A similar idea must have been entertained by Latin writers, who take Mavors to be qui magna vertit. But all this is extremely doubtful, if not impossible, on phonetic grounds.
Mamurius.
zh: 在神話學中亦是如此。例如，如庫恩（Kuhn）所指出的，若我們被告知魯陀羅（Rudra）的頭髮被描繪成辮起並紮成一個結（kapardin），而阿波羅的頭髮亦被描繪成以同樣的方式排列（ἀκέρκομος），這首先是並不完全正確的，其次它會證明過多的東西。凡我們所被允許的，以及我相信庫恩真正所指的，無非是說一位如同阿波羅般被描繪為迅速穿梭於空氣中移動的神，自然會被想像為頭髮蓬鬆地飛揚於頭部周圍，而這正是他在最古老的雕像中所被描繪的方式，儘管在……的情況下¹ --- ¹ K. Z., iii, 335；及馮‧施羅德（von Schroeder），K. Z., xxix, 227。

魯陀拉（Rudra）的髮髻或頂髻，形狀如貝殼，可能不過是他的信徒後來因濕婆（Śiva）的信徒所效仿的髮髻習俗的重複而已。若庫恩（Kuhn）將阿波羅（Apollon）的稱號洛克西亞斯（Loxias，斜向者）與魯陀拉的稱號凡庫（vanku，橫斜地）相比較，他幾乎不可能以λοξόϛ（彎曲的）之意來理解洛克西亞斯，進而將其視為暗指阿波羅神諭的彎曲或欺詐性質。這種對洛克西亞斯的詮釋早已被奧特弗里德‧穆勒（Otfried Müller）所駁斥，他證明了阿波羅的妹妹阿爾忒彌斯（Artemis）同樣被稱為洛克索（Loxo），儘管她從未被指控曾發表過任何神諭，無論是直言還是曲言。庫恩真正的意思是，應用於魯陀拉的凡庫（vañku）大概是為了表示猛烈地橫斜穿過空氣，而洛克西亞斯最初應用於阿波羅時也具有相同的物理意涵。但許多神祇都被描繪為運動或衝刺，卻並不因此就相同，況且凡庫也不是魯陀拉獨有的稱號。

關於庫恩（Kuhn）的評述也可以做同樣的說法，即阿波羅彈琴，且存在一種稱為「魯德里」的樂器，可能與 χύρα 是同一個詞。魯德里是一個很晚出的詞彙，在梵文文獻中的證據甚少。阿波羅作為繆斯女神領袖的音樂特性是太過於純粹的希臘風格，不足以與魯陀羅相比較。魯陀羅的音樂（如果有的話）更像是暴風雨的呼嘯和咆哮，而非琴弦之聲。不過，如果庫恩只是想指涉魯陀羅的物質性格質，他原本可以¹ ¹ M. M., S. B. E., vol. xxxii, p. 424.
Vi] 阿波羅。705 他援引盧陀羅（Rudras）之例來加強其論證，即摩樓荼（Maruts），亦即風暴之風，在吠陀中經常被描繪為歌者（arkin）¹。誠然，阿波羅與盧陀羅二者皆有弓與箭矢，但在吠陀及希臘神話中，其他眾神亦復如是。阿耆尼（Agni）不斷射出箭矢，阿爾忒彌斯、赫拉克勒斯及奧德修斯皆以射手聞名，雖則在古希臘人中，弓的使用被視為不若劍或矛那般光榮。
阿波羅與魯陀都被描繪為最傑出的醫者，這確實令人驚異，並表明如我們將看到的那樣，庫恩關於魯陀和阿波羅為同源神祇的理論中確有某些真理。然而庫恩所忽略考慮的是，阿波羅與魯陀之間許多差異之處，這些差異對於我們的目的同樣重要。

我們現在轉向檢視馮‧施羅德的觀點，即存在一位神祇阿波洪─阿格尼（Apohon-Agni），也就是說，吠陀詩人所稱的阿格尼（Agni）即為希臘人所稱的阿波羅（Apollon）。這種觀點無疑包含了一定程度的真理，只要阿格尼被理解為他在吠陀中經常出現的樣子，不僅僅是火、普通的火（ignis），而是天火或天光。這兩種光的表現形式——爐火上的光與東方的光——之間關係有多緊密，已經反復得以說明。因此，倘若馮‧施羅德將阿格尼理解不是在火的普通意義上，而是作為熾熱的太陽，更特別是升起的太陽，我將與他完全贊同。魯陀和阿格尼在吠陀中呈現給我們的形象發展已經太為成熟，無法¹ ¹ M. M., Vedic Hymns, S. B. E., vol. xxxii, p. 95.

我們必須追溯至吠陀諸神之前，才能希冀找到那些思想的根源，這些思想在希臘土地上產生了斐波斯‧阿波羅（Phoibos Apollon），在印度土地上產生了阿格尼（Agni）。apa-var 對於日出這一日常啟示的持續使用，表明在阿波羅之後以及在日出各種人格化之後，存在著一個共同的觀念——一個啟示者或晨曦提婆的觀念，這也是聖詩作者所說的永恆之門（Devir Dvara/i, or the Everlasting Gates）的萌芽。若從這一中心思想出發產生了與希臘的阿波羅之名相關聯的次生思想，或與梵文中的阿格尼（Agni）、蘇利耶（Surya）或魯陀羅（Rudra）之名相關聯的次生思想，那麼所有的巧合，即使是最微細且表面上看似無關緊要的巧合，都將是受歡迎的，因為它們表明了始於前吠陀時代的神話成長過程在印度和希臘遵循了同樣的自然路徑，呈現出許多相似之處，但也不妨礙希臘諸神與印度諸神之間的差異。阿波羅不能是魯陀羅（Rudra），阿格尼也不能是，正如希臘的統治者（Archon）不能是佛教的阿羅漢（Arhan），或甚至拉丁文的 rex 也不能是梵文的 rkg 一樣。
普德拉（Pudra）原本是風暴神，不管有什麼相反的說法。如果馬魯特斯（Maruts）是風暴神，即使在現代梵文中 marut 意為風，我們也不能忘記馬魯特斯不僅被稱為普德拉之子，而且被稱為普德拉本身。

當我們讀到魯陀羅（Pudra）是諸神中最美麗者，他持弓箭，是歌曲之主（gatha-pati），是最偉大的醫師，並能消除人類所犯的罪惡（VI, 74, 3）時，我們彷彿看到一位神祇，他確實令人想起阿波羅（Apollon）。但當魯陀羅被稱為兇悍的、四肢強健的神祇，如野獸般襲擊，當他被稱為紅色野豬，當在《阿闥婆吠陀》中他的腹部據說是藍色的、背部是紅色時，我們即便在阿波羅最可怕的形象中，也無法再辨識出他的特徵。更不可能將魯陀羅與魯陀羅們識別為單純的森林精靈與薩堤爾（Satyrs）、山林之靈，因為魯陀羅是一位雄偉的神祇，他如明亮的太陽般閃耀（I, 43, 5），是這廣大世界之主，神聖力量永不離他而去（II, 33, 9）。
希臘和義大利諸神
如果在提婆神祇在印度或希臘各自形成確定個性之後，企圖將吠陀神祇與希臘神祇相互認同是不可能的，更是不合理的，那麼對於那些在希臘與義大利各自形成確定性格的神祇，亦復如是。比較神話學所能做的，是試圖深入這類神祇之後，若有可能，探尋它們遠離義大利和希臘的共同淵源；但我們不能，也不應該嘗試追溯義大利神話中具體屬於希臘的特性。

我向來推崇羅舍爾（Roscher）多年前在一八七三年發表的《阿波羅與瑪爾斯》論文。該文充滿深邃的學識與明晰的洞察，其後更有其他著作承繼那輝煌的開端，足堪比肩。我至今也不否認這兩位神祇確有許多共通之處，然而我無法相信它們源於同一個根源。阿波羅誠如羅舍爾已充分論證的，乃是一位光明之神，一位 *choios* 〔明亮的、光耀的〕—— --- **註**：原文末尾似乎未完整，標注 "1 Oldenb erg, Rel. des Veda, p. 223." 為腳註出處，已保留。希臘文 *choios* 意為「明亮的」或「光耀的」。
708 希臘與義大利諸神。[章節]

奧瑞翁（Aureios）、阿波羅紐斯（Apolloneus）、亞歷克桑德（Alexios）、厄翁（Eōn）和厄南諾斯（Enanos），他同時也是年度之神，即太陽神；他更特別是春季與夏季之神，是戰鬥之神，也像大多數神祇一般，被視為各個家族、氏族和整個部落的先祖，是城市的守護神，殖民地的領袖。這一切自然源於他的太陽特性。他是阿帕－瓦良人（apa-varyan），但他並未在我們所知的吠陀中任何個別提婆中被個別化。因此，若我們如波歇爾（Poscher）所認為的，假設在某個時期內，這個尚未確定的阿帕－瓦良人在義大利神祇（後來被稱為或實際上被稱為馬爾斯）相同的神話思維流域中延續下來，我們就必須承認存在所謂且我相信被錯誤稱為的雅利安家族「希臘－義大利分支」長期連續、未曾分離的存在，以及一種與雅利安神話其他分支特性相異的希臘－義大利神話的發展。而在這個過程進行之際，我們就必須相信這些相關的希臘－義大利提婆中之一的名稱在希臘變成了阿波羅（Apollon），但在義大利卻變成了馬爾斯（Mars），即名稱改變了，但實質仍保持相同。我並不否認神祇可以在實質上相同，儘管名稱不同，但我懷疑任何無名的雅利安提婆之存在的可能性。我們在瓦魯納（Varuna）和阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahura Mazda）的例子中見到了清楚的證例，羅歇爾教授也提及了幾個類似的例子，如厄里倪斯（Erinyes）、歐美尼得斯（Eumenides）、阿剌埃（Arae）與塞姆奈（Semnae）；普路同（Plouton）、哈得斯（Hades）、克呂梅諾斯（Klymenos）；赫柏（Hebe）與伽尼梅得斯（Ganymedes）；阿佛羅狄蒂（Aphrodite）、庫普里斯（Kypris）與庫瑟瑞亞（Kythereia）；柏瑟芬妮（Persephone）、科瑞（Kore）與芬瑟芬塔（Phersephatta）。但這些例子並非都切於要點。我們必須區分神聖個體獲得眾多名稱的情況，與其中一個或多個名稱在時間推移中成為獨立名稱的情況。若阿佛羅狄蒂或阿佛羅革涅亞（Aphrogeneia）因在這些島嶼獲得特殊祭祀而被稱為庫瑟瑞亞與庫普里斯，且若後來她在這些名稱下顯現（無論它們曾是單純的修飾語或獨立名稱），似乎成為一位新的女神，我們可以清楚看到所謂她的實質保持相同，儘管她的名稱改變了。但這並非羅歇爾教授假設的阿波羅與馬爾斯的情況。若我理解無誤，他並不認為阿波羅曾獲得馬爾斯的修飾語，或馬爾斯曾獲得阿波羅的修飾語，而是相信這同一位尚未具名的提婆在希臘獲得了阿波羅的名稱，在義大利獲得了馬爾斯的名稱，即馬爾斯與阿波羅實際上是方言同義詞（dialectic synonyms）。在此我們必須首先設法清晰地思考這個問題。我們已見每一位提婆都以其名稱開始其存在。我主張即使太陽與月亮——對於作為思考者的人類而言——在被命名之前也並不存在；但至少赫利俄斯（Helios）與塞萊涅（Selene）在被造名者（ὀνοματοθέτης）創造之前不可能有任何存在。
從來沒有赫利俄斯（Helios）或阿波羅（Apollon）存在過，除非在某個人的心智裡——此人匯聚了某些感性的印象，領悟或構想了它們，並根據其突出的特徵之一為之命名，藉此創造出一位顯示之神，一位阿波羅。之後，可能發生兩件事。阿波羅可能獲得許多的別名，其中某些別名，如赫卡托斯（Hekatos）「遠達者」或……

赫卡忒波羅斯（Hekatebolos），遠射者，或德利奧斯（Delios），光明者或生於德洛斯者，可能獲得某種神話學上的獨立性；或其次，那些喚起阿波羅（Apollon）之名的相同印象，可能無須參照該名號，而喚起另一個名號和另一位神祇，其與阿波羅是如此接近，以至於兩者無法區分，久而久之便成為一體。以此方式，斐波斯（Phoibos），光彩奪目者，其所代表的概念與阿波羅相同至極，以至於兩者被並稱為斐波斯‧阿波羅（Phoibos Apollon），而斐波斯遂不再是獨立的神祇，嗣後被接納為阿波羅之純粹稱號，如赫卡托斯（Hekatos）一般，儘管其實際上乃由獨立的過程所產生。
如果我們問古希臘義大利的阿波羅與古希臘義大利的瑪爾斯之間的關係是什麼，我們不可能接受其中一個是另一個的別稱，但我們只能認為，接受阿波羅之名的那個概念同樣接受了瑪爾斯的名稱。這能被證明嗎？
阿波羅與瑪爾斯

博歇爾教授似乎沒有注意到，希臘人自己曾主張阿波羅與瑪爾斯的同一性。普魯塔克（*Fragm.* p. 15）辯論道，若兩位女神被表現為同一個孩子的母親，則兩者之間必然存有共同的要素。因此，他認為勒托不可能有別於赫拉，因為勒托是阿爾忒彌斯的母親，而阿爾忒彌斯在被稱為艾琉提亞（Eileithyia）時，乃是赫拉的女兒。隨後他繼而辯論，既然阿瑞斯與阿波羅具有相同的特性（*Swa/xis*），一者為赫拉之子，另一者為勒托之子，由此可推論，勒托與赫拉必須被視為同一位女神。

但這恐怕難以令羅歇爾（Roscher）教授滿意。儘管羅馬人被引導著將他們的馬爾斯與希臘的阿瑞斯相等同，他已恰當地指出（第14頁）這種等同可能發生於後來時期，並且出於誤解的觀點，而且這兩位神祇早在羅馬重新相聚前就已在性質上產生了分歧。因此問題在於，真正的羅馬馬爾斯是否展現出太陽起源的跡象，以及與阿波羅的先前同一性，以至於我們必須將希臘的阿波羅和拉丁的馬爾斯視為單純的異名，從一開始就被指向同一個對象。
馬爾斯之所以是眾光輝、光明施予的神祇之一，可由他被祭祀為盧克‧埃提烏斯（Leuc-etius）或盧克‧埃提烏斯（Louc-etius）而得以證明，而阿波隆的敬稱則為 Aὐχ́ων、Aὐχηει09、Aὐκ-θυεντή 等。

但儘管此一名號固然可以證明馬爾斯屬於光明之神、提婆之列，它也只能證明他是眾提婆中的一位、一個光明的存在，而不足以證明他是太陽神或與阿波羅（Apollon）同一。露切蒂烏斯（Lucetius）作為一個名號，同樣屬於朱庇特，也屬於馬爾斯，甚至對朱庇特更是如此。費斯圖斯說：「露切蒂烏姆‧約維姆‧阿佩拉巴特‧夸德‧埃烏姆‧路奇斯‧埃塞‧考薩姆‧克雷德巴特」（Lucetium Jovem appellabant quod eum lucis esse causam credebant），而據馬克羅比烏斯所言，沙利僧侶在其聖歌中所慶祝的正是這位露切蒂烏斯‧朱庇特，而非馬爾斯。這位露切蒂烏斯‧朱庇特的妻子被稱作露切蒂亞及露西娜（Lucina），而馬爾斯的妻子則是涅里亞（Neria）。
然而，雖然我們必須放棄火星與阿波羅同一性最有力的論據，¹ Hartung, Die Religion der Eomer, ii, 9.

即他的名字盧切提烏斯（Lucetius），我們仍然可以保留這樣的事實：瑪爾斯屬於提婆，他被認為能帶來光明，而且如我們將看到的，還帶來春天的溫暖。因此三月（Martius）是他的月份，即三月，這是義大利春天的開始。他實際上是一位純粹的義大利神祇，在他的所有特徵中都是義大利農民的神。首先談他的名字，我仍然主張，基於我在《吠陀讚歌導論》第三十二卷第二十四頁所述的理由，瑪爾斯（Mars, Martis）對應於馬魯特（Marut），風暴之風，原意為砸碎者或搗打者，如同皮盧姆努斯（Pilumnus）及其短矛（pilum），以及皮庫姆努斯（Picumnus），與聖木啄木鳥（Picus Martius）相關聯。馬魯特源自馬爾（mar，意為明亮）的舊有推導面臨著巨大的困難。如果這樣的語根確實屬於雅利安語族，它肯定在梵文中沒有留下任何後裔，甚至沒有 mari- 一詞。從馬爾推導而來（死亡之義）在梵文中也站不住腳，因為在獻給馬魯特們的讚歌中，沒有任何暗示他們代表亡靈，也幾乎沒有關於他們將這些靈魂引領到最後安息之地的任何內容。我們很少能發現如同吠陀的 sardha maruta（馬魯特兵團）與翁布里亞語的 cerfo Martio 之間這樣顯著的巧合，這確實應該已經解決了瑪爾斯與馬魯特之間聯繫的問題。風或颶風被提升為至高神祇在農民和牧羊人當中是非常自然的，同樣的過程不僅可以在胡拉坎（Hurakan）的情況下看到，在烏歐坦（Wuotan）的情況下同樣也能觀察到。風擁有所有的

那些在瑪爾斯的好戰性格中出現的特質，與摩樓陀（Maruts）的事實相符——即摩樓陀在吠陀中被表現為全副武裝的戰士——這一點在第一卷中可以輕易看出，該卷提供了所有現存的獻給摩樓陀的讚歌的譯文。但是，瑪爾斯作為風的代表應該被視為對田野、草地與森林有益，這則更難解釋。我們對三月風的觀念僅源自其有害的方面。但在南方氣候中，三月的風標誌著太陽與晴朗天氣的回歸。風本身被認為對於掃蕩田地、驅散殘留的落葉或積雪或塵埃、乾燥潮濕的土壤與淨化空氣、實際上清潔奧革亞斯（Augeias）的牲口棚都是必要的。因此德瓦拉（Deverra），掃除者，是皮盧姆努斯（Pilumnus）的恰當同伴，而皮庫斯或皮庫姆努斯（Picus or Picumnus）則因引入施肥而受到讚譽，兩者都是瑪爾斯的朋友與同伴，有時甚至是他的代理人。

皮庫斯之子、拉丁努斯之父，以及農業的守護神是法烏努斯。法烏努斯與法烏尼（Fauni）後來被同定為希臘的潘與潘尼斯。他們的名字通常被認為源自 favere（保護）一詞，但由於吠陀中存在一類稱為 Dhuni 的神靈存在，其意為「搖動者」或「呼喊者」，法烏努斯更有可能是源自 dhu 或 dhev，正如 δνέλλα 以及 fumus 之源，而非毫無意義的 favere（恩惠）。馬爾斯旁邊的 Mavors 這一形式難以解釋。Maurs 出現於銘文中，被莫姆森認為代表馬爾斯。
第714頁 阿波羅與馬爾斯 [章節]

殺人者。里奇爾（Ritschl）認為它代表馬沃爾斯（Mavors）；但什麼是馬沃爾斯？除非可以視之為單純的沃爾斯（Maurs）的延長形式（如同法沃尼烏斯之於法烏努斯），否則我看不出有任何可能解釋它。科爾森（Corssen）將其解釋為 mag-vors 的縮約形式，並認為 mag 可能是 fia-x7) 的拉丁形式。這樣便可得出「戰事轉向者」（battle-turner）的含義，如同希臘文中的 τροπαῖος（tropaios）。拉丁作家們似乎也懷有類似的想法，他們認為馬沃爾斯是「掌轉大事者」（qui magna vertit）。然而，若從音韻學的角度考量，所有這些解釋都極其可疑，甚至是不可能的。
瑪穆留斯（Mamurius）
